nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still forest streams, pools, and swampy margins with overhanging vegetation; typically shallow, clear to lightly tea-stained water near the surface

decorPreferences: floating plants (Amazon frogbit, water lettuce, salvinia) to diffuse surface light and provide cover
fine-leaved plants (java moss, hornwort) for spawning sites
gentle filtration — low surface turbulence preferred
tight-fitting lid essential due to strong escape tendency

diet.notes: The Lampeye Killifish is a micro-carnivore that requires small live or frozen invertebrates as the basis of its diet. Staples include live or frozen daphnia, baby brine shrimp, micro worms, and mosquito larvae. The fish will generally accept high-quality micro-pellets and crushed flake once settled, but tends to thrive best on a varied live and frozen diet. Its small mouth limits it to very small food particles, generally no larger than 1 to 2 mm; larger food items will simply be ignored or rejected.

goodWith: small peaceful West African tetras (e.g., Congo tetra juveniles, ember tetras)
peaceful dwarf cichlids kept in larger tanks
small freshwater shrimp (Neocaridina) in community setups with care
avoidWith: large or boisterous fish that outcompete at the surface
fast-swimming species that cause constant stress
predatory fish capable of swallowing small killifish
species requiring very soft acidic water exclusively (if hard-water forms)

summary: The Lampeye Killifish is a small West African surface-dweller named for its striking iridescent blue iris. Unlike many killifish relatives, it is non-annual with a lifespan of 2 to 4 years. It requires a tight-fitting lid, micro-sized foods, and calm tankmates, and tolerates moderately hard water.
careNotes: The Lampeye Killifish (Procatopus websteri) is a non-annual killifish — unlike the better-known Nothobranchius species, it does not require seasonal drying of eggs and has a lifespan of 2 to 4 years in captivity. The species belongs to family Procatopodidae, a group of small West African egg-scattering surface fish characterised by their striking iridescent blue or blue-green iris, from which the common name 'lampeye' derives. A well-fitted lid is non-negotiable: lampeyes are accomplished jumpers and can escape through the smallest gaps. A tank of 40 L is a workable minimum for a small shoal, but 60 to 80 L provides noticeably more stability and allows a larger group, which reduces stress and brings out more natural behaviour. Keep in groups of at least 6, ideally 8 to 10, as solitary individuals tend to become timid and rarely feed well. Floating plants are highly recommended to break up surface light and provide refuge; under full open-top brightness the fish tend to hide and colour poorly. Filtration should be gentle — a small sponge filter is ideal — as strong surface agitation stresses them. Water parameters are moderately flexible: the species is native to both soft forest streams and more mineralised coastal drainages in Cameroon and Nigeria, so some populations tolerate harder, mildly alkaline water (pH up to around 7.8, gH up to around 18) well. Extremes of either hardness or acidity should be avoided. Water changes of 20 to 30% weekly are important, as the fish are sensitive to dissolved waste buildup. Because of their small mouth, all foods must be appropriately sized; micro-pellets smaller than 1 mm, finely crushed flake, and small live or frozen invertebrates are essential. Dry foods alone lead to nutritional deficiencies over time. The reflective blue iris tends to appear most vivid in healthy, well-fed fish kept in well-lit but not harsh conditions.
breedingNotes: The Lampeye Killifish (Procatopus websteri) is an egg-scatterer that deposits adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants and java moss. Unlike annual killifish — which require their eggs to go through a dry dormancy period in nature — the eggs of this non-annual species do not need any diapause dry period and hatch in 10 to 14 days at 24°C. A dedicated breeding tank of 20 to 30 L with a thick java moss mat or spawning mop is recommended; adults should be removed after spawning, as they will eat the eggs. Feed the fry infusoria and paramecia (microscopic organisms used as first foods for very small fry) for the first few days, then gradually introduce baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms. Fry are tiny and require pristine, stable water. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 3 to 4 months. Males tend to be slimmer and more intensely coloured; females are slightly rounder-bodied. Breeding is considered moderately difficult — generally straightforward once a conditioned pair is established on live foods, but the fry stage requires careful attention to water quality.

Provided native range (Cameroon, Nigeria), adult size (~4 cm), temperature range, pH and hardness parameters, surface-dwelling behaviour, non-annual lifecycle, diet (small invertebrates), breeding (egg-scatterer, 10–14 day hatch), escape-risk warning, and minimum tank size guidance.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — African Lampeyes: Procatopodidae
additional.notes: Background on Procatopodidae taxonomy (historic placement within Poeciliidae vs current family-level status), reflective iris morphology (lampeye common name origin), habitat ecology across West African forest streams, and notes on hard-alkaline tolerance in coastal-drainage populations.

fish.breedingNotes: The Lampeye Killifish is an egg-scatterer that spawns among java moss or fine-leaved plants. Eggs hatch in 10 to 14 days without any dry period, as the species is non-annual. Fry are tiny and require infusoria or paramecia for the first few days before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Adults should be removed after spawning, as they will eat the eggs. Breeding is considered moderately difficult, primarily due to the delicate fry stage.
